title: Abdominal pain with evolving appendicitis differential

transcript.speaker: Clinician
transcript.text: Where is the abdominal pain now and when did it start?

transcript.speaker: Patient
transcript.text: It started near my belly button last night and now it is more on the lower right.

transcript.speaker: Clinician
transcript.text: Any vomiting, fever, diarrhoea, urinary symptoms, or pregnancy possibility?

transcript.speaker: Patient
transcript.text: I vomited once. No diarrhoea or urinary symptoms. I am on contraception and my period finished last week.

transcript.speaker: Clinician
transcript.text: On exam you are tender in the right lower abdomen with guarding. Your temperature is 37.8.

transcript.speaker: Clinician
transcript.text: Yes. Appendicitis is possible. You need ED/surgical assessment today. Do not eat or drink until reviewed. Go by private car with your partner now, or ambulance if pain worsens, fainting, or repeated vomiting.

candidate_notes.note: Migratory abdominal pain from periumbilical to right lower quadrant since last night. Vomited once. No diarrhoea or urinary symptoms. Pregnancy less likely by history but not formally excluded. Exam: RLQ tenderness with guarding, T 37.8. Impression possible appendicitis. Plan: ED/surgical assessment today, nil by mouth until review. Ambulance if worsening pain, fainting, or repeated vomiting.
Abdominal pain and one vomit. No diarrhoea or urinary symptoms. Likely gastroenteritis. Advised fluids, simple analgesia, and GP review if not improving.

S: Pain began periumbilical, now RLQ. Vomited once. No diarrhoea/urinary symptoms. Period finished last week, on contraception. O: T 37.8, RLQ tenderness with guarding. A: Possible appendicitis. P: ED/surgical assessment today, avoid oral intake until reviewed.

Possible appendicitis: migratory periumbilical to RLQ pain, vomiting once, low-grade temperature, RLQ guarding. No diarrhoea or urinary symptoms. Pregnancy not confirmed excluded; period last week and contraception noted. Needs same-day ED/surgical assessment. Nil by mouth. Ambulance if worsening pain, syncope, or repeated vomiting.

CDSS abdominal pain pathway: appendicitis risk due to migratory pain, RLQ tenderness/guarding, vomiting, T 37.8. Avoid premature gastroenteritis diagnosis. Same-day ED/surgical review, nil by mouth. Escalation advice: ambulance for worsening pain, fainting, repeated vomiting. Pregnancy status should be checked in ED despite lower likelihood by history.
